planting.note: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in moist to wet, slightly acidic, peaty or sandy soil in full sun to partial shade; black spruce is adapted to boggy, poorly drained, acidic soils of northern bogs and swamps where few other conifers grow. Extremely cold-hardy — one of the few trees that survives the transition zone to arctic tundra.

directions: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in moist to wet, slightly acidic, peaty or sandy soil in full sun to partial shade; black spruce is the dominant tree of the boreal bog landscape of northern North America — growing across Canada and the northern US in cold, acidic, waterlogged bogs and muskeg where its shallow root system tolerates the poorly oxygenated, saturated soils that exclude most other conifers. A slow-growing, narrow, densely crowned spruce with distinctive clusters of persistent old cones that remain on the tree for years, giving branches a 'beaded' appearance. The needles are short and dark blue-green. Important for wildlife, providing nesting habitat for many boreal birds. The inner bark was used by Indigenous peoples for food and medicine. A key species for northern bog and wetland ecological plantings, rain garden wet zones, and permaculture systems in cold climates. The dwarf cultivar 'Nana' (12–18 inches tall) is used in rock gardens and container plantings.

planting.note: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained to moist, strongly acidic, cool, humus-rich, rocky or sandy loamy soil in full sun; red spruce grows naturally at high elevations in the Appalachians and at lower elevations in northern New England and Atlantic Canada, requiring cool, moist, acidic, well-drained conditions.

directions: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained to moist, strongly acidic, cool, humus-rich, rocky or sandy loamy soil in full sun; red spruce is the characteristic conifer of the southern Appalachian high peaks — the dominant tree of spruce-fir forests above 5,500 feet elevation on the highest summits of the Great Smoky Mountains, Roan Mountain, and the Black Mountains of North Carolina and Tennessee. The 'red' name comes from the reddish-brown young branchlets and the reddish cast of the bark on older trees. The small, reddish-brown cones are distinctive. Widely harvested for pulpwood, resonance wood for violin soundboards (Stradivarius violins were made from European spruce, but red spruce has been used for guitar tops and other musical instruments for its exceptional acoustic properties). Suffered severely from acid rain damage at high elevations in the 20th century; restoration plantings have been underway since the 1990s. One of the most important species for high-elevation Appalachian forest ecology.

planting.note: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral or slightly alkaline, average, loamy or clay soil in full sun to partial shade; Serbian spruce is one of the most adaptable and ornamentally outstanding spruces — tolerating both acidic and alkaline soils, air pollution, and a wide range of growing conditions.

directions: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral or slightly alkaline, average, loamy soil in full sun to partial shade; Serbian spruce is one of the most beautiful and narrowly columnar spruces in cultivation — a naturally slender, spire-shaped tree with gracefully pendulous branchlets, dark green needles with conspicuous silvery-white stomatal bands on the undersides, and very small, dense cones. The narrowly pendulous, weeping-branched form creates an elegant vertical exclamation mark in the landscape. Native to a tiny relict population in a limestone canyon of the Drina River valley in western Serbia — one of the rarest conifers in the wild, with fewer than 60,000 wild trees. Ironically, it has proven one of the most adaptable spruces in cultivation, tolerating a wider range of soils (including alkaline) and air pollution than most spruces. One of the best spruces for urban conditions and for narrow garden spaces where a vertical, densely columnar form is desired. More adaptable than Norway spruce in warm summers.

planting.note: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moist to average, fertile to average, loamy or sandy soil in full sun to partial shade; oriental spruce grows naturally in mountain forests of the Caucasus and northeastern Turkey, making it tolerant of cold winters and moderate summer heat — more heat-tolerant than most spruces.

directions: Plant seeds or container-grown trees in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moist to average, fertile to average, loamy or sandy soil in full sun to partial shade; oriental spruce is considered by many horticulturists to be the most beautiful of all the spruces — the extremely short needles (only 1/4 to 1/3 inch, the shortest of all spruces) are tightly pressed along the branchlets, creating an unusually dense, lustrous, deep-green, almost 'lacquered' appearance quite different from other, coarser-needled spruces. The small, narrowly cylindrical, violet-purple cones ripen to reddish-brown and are very ornamental. The spring growth of new bright lime-green needles contrasting with the dark older foliage is striking. More heat-tolerant than Norway spruce or Colorado blue spruce, adapting reasonably well to the warmer parts of its zone range. The cultivar 'Skylands' with permanently golden-yellow foliage is one of the most sought-after ornamental conifers in the nursery trade. An outstanding specimen tree for larger landscape settings.

planting.note: Plant seeds in fall for natural stratification or plant container-grown trees in spring in very well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry to average, rocky or sandy, lean soil in full sun; alligator juniper is highly drought tolerant once established, growing naturally on dry, rocky mountain slopes and canyon walls of the Southwest at 4,500–8,000 feet elevation.

directions: Plant container-grown trees in spring in very well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, dry to average, rocky or sandy, lean soil in full sun; alligator juniper is unmistakable — the only juniper with distinctive, deeply checkered, rectangular bark plates that perfectly resemble the textured, scaly skin of an alligator, making it one of the most easily identified conifers in the field. Native to the rocky, dry mountain slopes of southeastern Arizona, New Mexico, western Texas, and northern Mexico (the sky island mountain ranges and the Sierra Madre foothills) at mid-to-high elevations. One of the longer-lived junipers, with ancient specimens estimated at 500–1,000 years old. The blue-glaucous, berry-like seed cones are larger than most junipers. One of the drought-toughest conifers for the desert Southwest and Chihuahuan Desert transition zone — an excellent landscape tree for hot, dry, rocky sites where nearly nothing else survives. The dramatic bark texture makes it one of the most distinctive ornamental trees for dry southwestern landscapes.
